title: Antarctica's Blood Falls: Why a Glacier Bleeds Red
description: Taylor Glacier in Antarctica oozes red water from a crack in the ice — and the cause was locked away for over a million years. The iron-rich brine comes from an ancient subglacial lake sealed in total darkness, where iron slowly leached from bedrock with no oxygen. A 2017 study by Jill Mikucki confirmed the source using geochemical tracing. #Antarctica #BloodFalls #Geography #Science #TaylorGlacier

segments.voiceover: A glacier in Antarctica bleeds red — and the water pouring out has been trapped underground for over a million years.

It's called Blood Falls, on Taylor Glacier in the McMurdo Dry Valleys. Beneath it sits a saltwater lake, sealed under ice for around 1.5 million years. In 2017, geochemist Jill Mikucki confirmed the red color comes from iron in that brine — iron that slowly dissolved from bedrock in a completely oxygen-free environment.

The moment that iron-rich water hits open air, it rusts. Instantly. So the glacier isn't bleeding — it's oxidizing in real time. And that sealed lake underneath? It still contains living microbes, surviving with no light, no oxygen, and almost no heat.
Follow for one real fact a day.
